summary: Fail: the wrapper is wired into scoop creation correctly, but it still leaves two allow-list bypasses. Quoted command substitution/backticks and unsplit shell separators (newline and bare `&`) can execute disallowed commands while the wrapper only sees an allowed head.

issues.description: Quoted command substitution is still executable under a restricted allow-list. `findSubshellSyntax()` ignores `$()` and backticks while inside quotes, so an allowed head like `echo` can smuggle a disallowed command via `echo "$(curl http://x)"` or `echo "`curl http://x`"`.
issues.evidence: packages/webapp/src/tools/bash-tool-allowlist.ts:182-205 explicitly skips subshell detection while `quote` is set and even documents that `$(...)` inside double quotes is not caught. The feature contract requires rejecting subshell syntax (`validation-contract.md`: 371-380). The tests only cover unquoted forms at packages/webapp/tests/tools/bash-tool-allowlist.test.ts:206-220.
issues.suggestedFix: Treat command substitution as active inside double quotes (and reject backticks there too), then add regression tests for quoted `$()` and quoted backticks.

issues.description: The wrapper does not enforce the allow-list for all shell command separators. Because it only splits on `|`, `&&`, `||`, and `;`, a disallowed command can run after an allowed head via bare `&` or a newline in the same bash invocation.
issues.evidence: packages/webapp/src/tools/bash-tool-allowlist.ts:284-286 and 329-331 explicitly leave bare `&` inside a segment, and `splitSegments()` never handles newlines. `extractHead()` then stops at the first whitespace (390-400), so `ls\ncurl http://x` is accepted as head `ls`. packages/webapp/src/tools/bash-tool.ts:77-95 describes a full shell and forwards the raw string to `shell.executeCommand(command, signal)`, so those extra separators are still executed by bash.
issues.suggestedFix: Either parse or conservatively reject other command-separator constructs that the shell executes (at least bare `&` and newlines), and add regression tests covering `allowed & disallowed` and newline-separated commands.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The architecture note for the allow-list wrapper only mentions `|`, `&&`, `||`, `;` and generic subshell rejection. It should explicitly call out that the bash tool executes a full shell, so background `&`, newlines, and command substitution inside double quotes are also executable forms that must be parsed or rejected.
sharedStateObservations.why: The current shared-state description matches the implementation's blind spots, so a future worker could follow the documented design and still leave the same bypasses in place.
